slides.title: A BCP or Applicability Statement for Confidentiality and Authenticity Protection for Internet Email
PARCEP: A Protocol for Parental Controls
Taxonomy for Agentic AI Use Cases

chat.author: Hans-Jörg Happel
chat.text: Hi! I like the new (?) dispatch intro slides, Jim!

chat.author: Jim Fenton
chat.text: Thanks!

chat.author: Richard Barnes
chat.text: there's still TDM out there?

chat.author: Richard Barnes
chat.text: we should do nothing to support TDM

chat.author: Richard Barnes
chat.text: this seems like a big hack to avoid deploying STIR

chat.author: Eric Rescorla
chat.text: I'm also concerned that it doesn't work

chat.author: Pete Resnick
chat.text: Sounds more like routing around folks who refuse to deploy STIR.

chat.author: Richard Barnes
chat.text: It's a no from me, dawg

chat.author: Pete Resnick
chat.text: What's the downside (leaving aside ekr's point that it might not work)?

chat.author: Eric Rescorla
chat.text: There are a couple of problems. I'm imagining JDR is going to raise some of them

chat.author: Richard Barnes
chat.text: @ekr i share that concern.  if someone can spoof caller ID, they're probably in the vouching path as well

chat.author: Sean Turner
chat.text: :wait_one_second:

chat.author: Eric Rescorla
chat.text: Yeah this is the issue I raised.

chat.author: Jon Peterson
chat.text: All dial back auth mechanism have the same class of problems - reflection attacks and divergence of forward routing from originating authority

chat.author: Michael StJohns
chat.text: Did this get presented to STIR?

chat.author: Phillip Hallam-Baker
chat.text: It might be possible to design a protocol to do this but there is no chance of getting it deployed in the legacy telephone system.

chat.author: Jon Peterson
chat.text: Mike - in 2018 yeah

chat.author: Sean Turner
chat.text: call setup time is impacted .... so .....

chat.author: Michael StJohns
chat.text: Mumble

chat.author: Pete Resnick
chat.text: And if you're going to do the extension to prevent rings, you might as well deploy STIR, right?

chat.author: Jonathan Hoyland
chat.text: What if the call ostensibly comes from a non-local or high toll number? Do you get billed for a very short call?

chat.author: Phillip Hallam-Baker
chat.text: Stop trying to fix the telephone system, you can't add security to a system designed without it.

chat.author: David Rogers
chat.text: There have been a lot of things going on at GSMA, Open Verified Calling etc - it seems maybe better to start there?

chat.author: Muhammad Usama Sardar
chat.text: @Eric Rescorla can you speak slowly, please?

chat.author: Phillip Hallam-Baker
chat.text: The difficulty of deploying this update is greater than the difficulty of deploying a new open service protocol for voice.

chat.author: John Klensin
chat.text: PHB: One could say the same thing about email, of course.

chat.author: Phillip Hallam-Baker
chat.text: John, I say exactly that in response to email fixes. OpenPGP and S/MIME have been tried for 30 years without success.

chat.author: Aaron Parecki
chat.text: ekr if you wear a headset with a mic it'll be easier to hear you even at your normal speaking rate :-)

chat.author: Eric Rescorla
chat.text:
Aaron Parecki said:

ekr if you wear a headset with a mic it'll be easier to hear you even at your normal speaking rate :-)

Thanks for the feedback, but I actually am using a high quality podcast mic.

chat.author: Phillip Hallam-Baker
chat.text: John, moreover, replacing the telephone system alone is probably impossible, replacing email alone is probably impossible. But prospects for a protocol that does all the communication things people want to do in a single package could reach critical mass.

chat.author: Aaron Parecki
chat.text: Move it way closer to your face then, those need to be less than a foot away and I didn't see it on camera

chat.author: Jonathan Lennox
chat.text: It's possible the in-room playback of remote audio needs tweaking, you were the first remote speaker.

chat.author: Eric Rescorla
chat.text:
Aaron Parecki said:

Move it way closer to your face then, those need to be less than a foot away and I didn't see it on camera

It really is within a foot of my face. You just can't see it because of the way I have my camera.

chat.author: Jonathan Hoyland
chat.text: Yeah, the in-room sound isn't especially well tuned so far. I was struggling to hear the speaker a bit.

chat.author: Pete Resnick
chat.text: Jon's pointing out that we might need a DISPATCH wiki entry for "Why not to propose things like X".

chat.author: Pete Resnick
chat.text: Can we say that he volunteered to write one for this?

chat.author: Laura Scalone
chat.text: @Eric Rescorla, I wasn't able to write your question in time, could you write it down here or write it down in the Document for dispatch?

chat.author: Deb Cooley
chat.text: (or he could add it to the notes tool)

chat.author: Jonathan Hoyland
chat.text: @meetecho could we possibly increase the gain on the speaker mic?

chat.author: Eric Rescorla
Richard Barnes
chat.text: solution here is simple, just use Apple products for everything

chat.author: Lorenzo Miniero
chat.text: @Jonathan Hoyland do you mean in the local room, or in Meetecho? The current speaker sounds fine to us

chat.author: Jonathan Hoyland
chat.text: @Lorenzo In the local room. The speaker seems much quieter than the chairs.

chat.author: Aaron Parecki
chat.text: @richard :joy::joy::skull:

chat.author: Lorenzo Miniero
chat.text: Ack, I'll ping the AV team

chat.author: Richard Barnes
chat.text: This seems super locked in on a specific identity model

chat.author: Jim Fenton
chat.text: Maybe it's just the chairs are too loud :)

chat.author: Pete Resnick
chat.text: @Richard Barnes Yeah, except us goofballs who use MailMate on MacOS don't get the functionality.

chat.author: Jim Fenton
chat.text: Pete +1

chat.author: Richard Barnes
chat.text: @Pete user error, need to use Mail.app

chat.author: Naquib Tajudin
chat.text: Thanks Lorenzo, I find it a bit soft in Meetecho too.

chat.author: Dennis Jackson
chat.text: This draft says email a lot, but I don't see anything in the draft that's actually email specific. It'd work for any domain-scoped identifier. e.g. bluesky:dennis_jackson@bsky.app.

chat.author: Eric Rescorla
chat.text:
Dennis Jackson said:

This draft says email a lot, but I don't see anything in the draft that's actually email specific. It'd work for any domain-scoped identifier. e.g. bluesky:dennis_jackson@bsky.app.

It's not @dennis_jackson.bsky.app? :)

chat.author: Richard Barnes
chat.text: this is also super duplicative with OpenID Connect

chat.author: Dennis Jackson
chat.text: Who knows? Identifiers are hard :-P

chat.author: Pete Resnick
chat.text: @Richard Barnes Done only by amateur email users. :wink:

chat.author: Eric Rescorla
chat.text: app.bsky.dennis_jackson

chat.author: Richard Barnes
chat.text: dennis_jackson.mozilla.com

chat.author: Dennis Jackson
chat.text: Also the choice to use key-binding seems a little weird (over forwarding the challenge from the RP to the issuer directly). The current design doesn't achieve unlinkability or freshness and I think you want at least one if not both.

chat.author: Jim Reid
chat.text: IIUC ICANN doesn't allow underscores in TLDs. :-)

chat.author: Eric Rescorla
chat.text: how many organizations don't want to have the user's identity at the end of the day

chat.author: Eric Rescorla
chat.text: Like for password reset and the like?

chat.author: Stephen Farrell
chat.text: A picture would've been nice here:-)

chat.author: Martin Thomson
chat.text: The architecture isn't exactly relevant.

chat.author: Phillip Hallam-Baker
chat.text: Like the previous proposal, I see no chance of it being effectively deployed.

chat.author: Martin Thomson
chat.text: Because we all know that this is possible.

chat.author: Martin Thomson
chat.text: The question is whether it is a good idea to do.

chat.author: Eric Rescorla
chat.text: So what is it that the RP ends up knowing? Like "this person has a valid email address at domain X"?

chat.author: Jonathan Hoyland
chat.text:
Dennis Jackson said:

Also the choice to use key-binding seems a little weird (over forwarding the challenge from the RP to the issuer directly). The current design doesn't achieve unlinkability or freshness and I think you want at least one if not both.

Are you suggesting PrivacyPass?

chat.author: Richard Barnes
chat.text: @ekr i have actually encountered several websites that don't have a password/passkey flow, just a "send an email" flow

chat.author: Eric Rescorla
chat.text: Like it can't be unlinkable if the email address is eventually disclosed

chat.author: Jonathan Hoyland
Martin Thomson
chat.text: I'm not sure about the value of unlinkability.  I mean, it's pretty useful in some cases, but what would you seek to protect, and from whom?

chat.author: Phillip Hallam-Baker
chat.text: @Richard, me too. If you are always going to demand a callback, no need for a password.

chat.author: Aaron Parecki
chat.text: Many websites have only a "send an email" flow, also for account recovery

chat.author: Richard Barnes
chat.text: "we don't have time" -- means this is presented as a fait accompli

chat.author: Eric Rescorla
chat.text: Well, if we can't change anything then I think there's no point in dispatching it

chat.author: Pete Resnick
chat.text: It definitely ain't MAILMAINT. AD sponsored is a lovely idea from the POV of someone who is not an AD.

chat.author: Daniel Gillmor
chat.text: +1 Richard

chat.author: Ted Hardie
chat.text: I don't think is appropriate for ad-sponsored; the number of moving parts is too high.  Driving this to meet a specific deadline seems an inappropriate constraint.'

chat.author: Stephen Farrell
chat.text: so they're being forced to ask here by w3c I guess, but don't really wanna?

chat.author: Phillip Hallam-Baker
chat.text: +100 Richard

chat.author: Pete Resnick
chat.text: If you don't have time for us to work on it, then let's not.

chat.author: Kathleen Moriarty
chat.text: Sounds like v2 could have changes, not v1, similar to when other work has been transferred in

chat.author: Richard Barnes
chat.text: @Kathleen there is no "transfer in" here, this is new work

chat.author: Martin Thomson
chat.text:
Eric Rescorla said:

Well, if we can't change anything then I think there's no point in dispatching it

Well, clearly we should dispatch it to "nowhere" in that case.

chat.author: Eric Rescorla
chat.text:
Martin Thomson said:

Eric Rescorla said:

Well, if we can't change anything then I think there's no point in dispatching it

Well, clearly we should dispatch it to "nowhere" in that case.

Well, yes

chat.author: Eric Rescorla
chat.text: Wait, wait, how do you read email without a browser?

chat.author: Pete Resnick
chat.text: Heh

chat.author: Eric Rescorla
chat.text:
Eric Rescorla said:

Wait, wait, how do you read email without a browser?

Like do you print out your email?

chat.author: Martin Thomson
chat.text: The protocol, as I understand it, is not strictly browser-specific.  It's just better suited to clients that have all the same tools.

chat.author: Phillip Hallam-Baker
chat.text: Reminds me of DNS over QUIC which came out a decade after I proposed essentially the same thing to the DPRIV  WG kickoff meeting but was told 'we got to do this in 12 months so we have to use TLS and TCP-fast start'.

chat.author: Martin Thomson
chat.text:
Eric Rescorla said:

Eric Rescorla said:

Wait, wait, how do you read email without a browser?

Like do you print out your email?

I ask my EA to do that.  What do you do?

chat.author: Kathleen Moriarty
chat.text: When tech transfer happens, the v1 of the outside group has been brought in through the ISE or AD sponsored with tech transfer terms for change control to be handed over. Then the next version changes. I did this with PKCS.

chat.author: Richard Barnes
chat.text: @ekr i just have my assistant print it out and read it to me.  i don't know what he uses.

chat.author: Richard Barnes
chat.text: in case anyone is wondering why it takes me a while to reply to emails

chat.author: Kathleen Moriarty
chat.text: And for other specifications as an AD in coordination with the then Trust

chat.author: Richard Barnes
chat.text: @Kathleen again -- there is no transfer here

chat.author: Martin Thomson
chat.text: has anyone considered that maybe the friction is a feature worth keeping?

chat.author: Dennis Jackson
chat.text: die email die

chat.author: Ted Hardie
chat.text: The email the?

chat.author: Kathleen Moriarty
chat.text: Richard, we disagree from my past experience. If they are willing to hand over change control for the next version, then there is a transfer opportunity

chat.author: Jonathan Hoyland
chat.text:
Dennis Jackson said:

die email die

I think the standards compliant format is "email die die die"

chat.author: Richard Barnes
chat.text: we're not talking about the next version, we're talking about the first version

chat.author: Stephen Farrell
chat.text: +1 to BoF/focused WG

chat.author: Kathleen Moriarty
chat.text: Raising possibilities helps more than just shutting down work, they may want to consider options. Dick clarified - they are open to change

chat.author: Thom Wiggers
chat.text: I want to understand the security and privacy questions here and I think a BoF would be better here if it goes forward in IETF

chat.author: Martin Thomson
chat.text: A two year effort would be optimistic.

chat.author: Jonathan Hoyland
chat.text: @Thom Wiggers there is a BoF later today.

chat.author: Dennis Jackson
chat.text: @Jonathan Hoyland Side meeting

chat.author: Thom Wiggers
chat.text: *side meeting

chat.author: Thom Wiggers
chat.text: also I'm not in Vienna :(

chat.author: Stephen Farrell
chat.text: doesn't necessarily need a BoF I'd say

chat.author: Carolina Caeiro
chat.text: +1 Martin

chat.author: Pete Resnick
chat.text: You want fast, virtual BoF is fine, but this really has to be a WG.

chat.author: Jonathan Hoyland
chat.text: Do we have remote for side meetings this time? Sometimes there is.

chat.author: Martin Thomson
chat.text: Yeah, before this discussion, I was in the "form a WG from charter and no BoF" view, but the discussion changed my mind and there was no other way to deal with this other than a full BoF.

chat.author: Muhammad Usama Sardar
chat.text:
Jonathan Hoyland said:

Do we have remote for side meetings this time? Sometimes there is.

One can always join side meetings online (if the host allows).

chat.author: Martin Thomson
chat.text: And if Chromium wants to YOLO this (as is distressingly common), that's on them.

chat.author: Jonathan Lennox
chat.text: It's up to the organizer to arrange remote participation for side meetings; it's not provided by the IETF.  But they usually do.

chat.author: Jonathan Hoyland
chat.text: @Muhammad Usama Sardar not always. A few times the side meetings have been in rooms with no AV setup.

chat.author: Eric Rescorla
chat.text: This is a fairly bad misrepresentation of the last call comments

chat.author: Martin Thomson
chat.text: This slide is a complete mischaracterization of the objections to the draft.

chat.author: Pete Resnick
chat.text: @Richard Barnes Did you mean to turn on your video?

chat.author: Richard Barnes
chat.text: this is totally false

chat.author: Richard Barnes
chat.text: yes because i want to interject

chat.author: Dan York
chat.text:
Jonathan Hoyland said:

Do we have remote for side meetings this time? Sometimes there is.

Yes, the side meeting rooms are equipped with remote access. Side meeting hosts can choose to use that access or provide their own.

chat.author: Eric Rescorla
chat.text: And honestly, the premise here that the problem is that John Klensin understands email and the people objecting don't is also wrong

chat.author: Muhammad Usama Sardar
chat.text:
Jonathan Hoyland said:

Muhammad Usama Sardar not always. A few times the side meetings have been in rooms with no AV setup.

Maybe previously, but for the last 4 years, I've had public side meetings. That was never the case. I have seen nice AV setup in both rooms for this meeting. They have new laptops too :tada:

chat.author: Richard Barnes
chat.text: there is no misunderstanding of security / interop tradeoffs

chat.author: Martin Thomson
chat.text: The problem is that the draft forbids an implementation from choosing not to accept email over a secure channel.

chat.author: Eric Rescorla
chat.text: I really have no understanding of what the point of this presentation is

chat.author: Pete Resnick
chat.text: @Martin Thomson That's also false

chat.author: Richard Barnes
chat.text: @Pete don't make me copy/paste the text

chat.author: Pete Resnick
chat.text: Not for this forum.

chat.author: Martin Thomson
chat.text:
Pete Resnick said:

Martin Thomson That's also false

Really, if we can't agree on facts, then we can't get to the actual disagreement.

chat.author: Richard Barnes
chat.text: "SMTP-receivers MUST NOT require SMTP-senders to use over-the-wire confidentiality mechanisms"

chat.author: Richard Barnes
chat.text: it is for this forum given that this preso is grounded in a misunderstanding of the misunderstandings in the discussion of that draft

chat.author: Phillip Hallam-Baker
chat.text: Again, I see no reasonable expectation of success. SMTP email is worn out, the protocol cannot be fixed. Every change to fix one hole creates more.

chat.author: Eric Rescorla
chat.text:
Richard Barnes said:

"SMTP-receivers MUST NOT require SMTP-senders to use over-the-wire confidentiality mechanisms"

The original text prohibited implementations from requiring TLS without a configuration knob disabling that.  The new text now prohibits endpoints from requiring TLS at all.

chat.author: Stephen Farrell
chat.text: Unless there's someone who wants to do the work JCK is proposing be done by someone else, then doesn't this have to GOTO /dev/null?

chat.author: Eric Rescorla
chat.text:
Stephen Farrell said:

Unless there's someone who wants to do the work JCK is proposing be done by someone else, then doesn't this have to GOTO /dev/null?

The mic is yours

chat.author: Pete Resnick
chat.text: @Richard Barnes The proposal is completely independent of that discussion (some of John's presentation about motivations with which I disagree nothwithstanding).

chat.author: Ted Hardie
chat.text: A BoF judges whether or not there are enough people to do the work.  So boF it and see.

chat.author: Andrew Campling
chat.text: Just because the draft says x doesn't mean that any dispatch destination can't change / ignore it

chat.author: Jonathan Hoyland
chat.text: Is the ask for a BCP draft?

chat.author: Stephen Farrell
chat.text: @ted: but who're the BoF proponents?

chat.author: Pete Resnick
chat.text: BoF is the correct output for this.

chat.author: Jim Reid
chat.text: It's not clear to me what the ask is.

chat.author: Martin Thomson
chat.text: I too have no idea what JCK is asking for

chat.author: Stephen Farrell
chat.text: i got out of the queue as ekr made my point

chat.author: Andrew Campling
chat.text: Disptach to mailing list first to establish interest?

chat.author: Shuping Peng
chat.text: which mailing list?

chat.author: Pete Resnick
chat.text: I'm not clear that BoFing for what would in the end be a BCP is going to be successful, but a BoF, virtual or in-person, is the correct outcome for this.

chat.author: Pete Resnick
chat.text: Create a mailing list

chat.author: Richard Barnes
chat.text: i predict that there will be no energy to do any of this

chat.author: Daniel Gillmor
chat.text: Did Richard just ask for https://xkcd.com/927/ already?

chat.author: Andrew Campling
chat.text: @Shuping a new mailing list unless there is a relevant existing one?

chat.author: Richard Barnes
chat.text: @DKG i was thinking more "don't do this"

chat.author: Eliot Lear
chat.text: @dkg have you MEMORIZED that URL? ;-)

chat.author: Daniel Gillmor
chat.text: it comes up every IETF, just not usually this early

chat.author: Ted Hardie
chat.text: @Eliot. I have it as a keyboard macro

chat.author: Eliot Lear
chat.text: :grinning:

chat.author: Jonathan Hoyland
chat.text: I think this is the key point. My number 1 requirement for email is that my email gets delivered.

chat.author: Richard Barnes
chat.text: i am in favor of finishing email

chat.author: Eric Rescorla
chat.text:
Richard Barnes said:

i predict that there will be no energy to do any of this

Well that's why I want to have some sort of temperature check before we reserve a BOF slot

chat.author: Richard Barnes
chat.text: i actually don't think there's any trade-off with deliverability here

chat.author: Jim Reid
chat.text: Finishing in the sense of completing the protocol or finishing it off with a coup de grace?

chat.author: Richard Barnes
chat.text: @Jim Reid Yes

chat.author: Jim Reid
chat.text: That's clear - thanks Richard.

chat.author: Thom Wiggers
chat.text: what are you guy's inboxes like that you're saying "this email could have been a meeting"

chat.author: Yoav Nir
chat.text: Getting rid of email would be 10 times as hard as getting rid of IPv4

chat.author: John Levine
chat.text: Email has regularly been declared dead for the past three decades. No need to stop now.

chat.author: Jonathan Hoyland
chat.text:
Richard Barnes said:

i actually don't think there's any trade-off with deliverability here

I was referring to the comment at the mic that "we can't change stuff without breaking stuff"

chat.author: Pete Resnick
chat.text: @Yoav Nir +10

chat.author: Jonathan Lennox
chat.text: Light gray on white, not great slide design

chat.author: Ted Hardie
chat.text: Based on the draft, this is an entire set of interlocking relationships, formats, and protocol elements.  So it is an architecture rather than a single protocol proposal.

chat.author: John Klensin
chat.text: @Yoav: Disagree.  Must more than 10 times :-(.   Remember that email predates IPv4 and, as John L mentioned, it is _really_ entrenched.

chat.author: Jonathan Hoyland
chat.text:
Jonathan Lennox said:

Light gray on white, not great slide design

I wonder if we could add an accessibility check on slide upload and nudge people to have better contrast / colour selection etc.

chat.author: John Klensin
chat.text: s/Must/Much/

chat.author: Robert Moskowitz
chat.text: @JonathanLennox:  A very comforting design for those of us working 4am...

chat.author: Stephen Farrell
chat.text: so that per-household MLS group is called thought-police maybe?

chat.author: Richard Barnes
chat.text: allow me to raise the usual: Would the interested vendors please stand up?

chat.author: Eric Rescorla
chat.text: To the mioc

chat.author: Eric Rescorla
chat.text: To the mic, Richard

chat.author: Ted Hardie
chat.text: @Stephen. "Brother", I think.

chat.author: Vittorio Bertola
chat.text: I can stand up but I do not want to monopolize the mic!

chat.author: Jonathan Hoyland
chat.text: This seems to suggest that there is a single canonical source of authority (or unit household) for a given child. I think reality is somewhat more complicated.

chat.author: Sophie Schmieg
chat.text: I have to say these systems did teach me a lot about computer security at a young age

chat.author: Dennis Jackson
chat.text: I read the draft and I can't work out how the trust anchors play into this. There seems to be some idea that everyone (parents and dependants) has digital credentials. Those credentials allow confirming a parent -> dependant relationship. And that it somehow used in the policy decision making to placce controls on the policies that parents can set? (???)

chat.author: Daniel Gillmor
chat.text: Sophie++

chat.author: Richard Barnes
chat.text:
Sophie Schmieg said:

I have to say these systems did teach me a lot about computer security at a young age

Exactly how I first took an interest in security!

chat.author: Eliot Lear
chat.text: Interesting IETF factoid: in 1998, Milo Medin had parental control systems in mind when he worked with William Randolf Hearst III to form @Home.

chat.author: John Klensin
chat.text: @Martin: I'm asking for a BCP or Applicability Statement that would draw things together, explain relationships and tradeoffs, and make recommendations.  Take a quick look at the skeleton I-D, which should clarify that.  And a BOF seems like a fine way to move forward.   If we conclude that the IETF doesn't care or doesn't have energy to work on this, that is useful data.

chat.author: Stephen Farrell
chat.text: can we dispatch to ITU-T? nice! :_)

chat.author: Vittorio Bertola
chat.text: @Stephen I think privacy etc will be better guaranteed by working on this at the IETF!

chat.author: Ted Hardie
chat.text: @Vittorio that's what Raven decided

chat.author: Eric Rescorla
chat.text: I also had trouble understanding this. It seemed like somehow the guardians need to be authorized by some third party. I think this is Ted Hardie's question

chat.author: Sophie Schmieg
chat.text: I'm also very worried about the societal implications about these solutions. It's used more than enough time to keep children from learning about LGBT topics

chat.author: Harald Alvestrand
chat.text: "child with mother", "child with father" and "child with grandparent" has different permission profiles in Real Life, so why not require that to be mirrored here?

chat.author: Eric Rescorla
chat.text: I certainly agree that there is not an existing WG

chat.author: Daniel Gillmor
chat.text: not to mention "child with babysitter" or "child with cousin"

chat.author: Harald Alvestrand
chat.text: Of course, we're all children from the perspective of certain lawmakers.

chat.author: Jonathan Hoyland
chat.text: Or emancipated child

chat.author: Jim Reid
chat.text: IETF in loco parentis?

chat.author: Eric Rescorla
chat.text: Practically all age assurance solutions don't try to deal with emancipated children other than to treat them as children

chat.author: Thom Wiggers
chat.text: certainly every child will claim emancipation to access roblox after hours

chat.author: Richard Barnes
chat.text: i agree that this proposal is loco

chat.author: Eric Rescorla
chat.text: I thought there was an MLS-group per household

chat.author: Thom Wiggers
chat.text: I don't know what "MLS-group per household" _means_ but I think I got that too

chat.author: Richard Barnes
chat.text: the field of "let's express human preferences in an interoperable protocol" is littered with corpses

chat.author: Eliot Lear
chat.text: I don't know about the solution.  The problem is perennial.  It's related to age verification, and maybe this is an issue for the IAB to consider.

chat.author: Richard Barnes
chat.text: tag yourself!  i'm GEOPRIV

chat.author: Thom Wiggers
chat.text: Every family has a domain name right, can't we just put something in .well-known

chat.author: Eric Rescorla
chat.text:
Thom Wiggers said:

I don't know what "MLS-group per household" _means_ but I think I got that too

So all the devices in the household somehow synchronize policies.

chat.author: Ted Hardie
chat.text: @Richard by showing our scars?

chat.author: Daniel Gillmor
chat.text: are we pretending that every child has exactly one family?

chat.author: Eric Rescorla
chat.text:
This is not at all related to what jurisdictions are 
Daniel Gillmor said:

are we pretending that every child has exactly one family?

Well, I think every device is affiliated with one household, probably

chat.author: Stephen Farrell
chat.text: this proposal is not IMO "much better" than what politicians are likely to propose

chat.author: Jonathan Hoyland
chat.text:
Daniel Gillmor said:

are we pretending that every child has exactly one family?

Exactly one family where all guardians agree.

chat.author: Sophie Schmieg
chat.text: It's one of those things where engineers have a professional obligation to not do a good job at it.

chat.author: Richard Barnes
chat.text: does the doc say how you tell what jurisdiction you're in?

chat.author: Eric Rescorla
chat.text:
Richard Barnes said:

does the doc say how you tell what jurisdiction you're in?

Probably not. But like let's not pretend that age assurance systems aren't gonna be widely deployed that assume they can figure out what jurisdiction they are in

chat.author: Phillip Hallam-Baker
chat.text:
For young children, the only viable approach to curation is whitelisting.
For older children, trying to do anything like this is futile.

chat.author: Kathleen Moriarty
chat.text: Scoping to IETF specific/appropriate work and support to implement would be necessary before a BoF. At most a mailing list for now to organize that. It also has to be easy as not many parents are able to use today’s answers to this space.

chat.author: Stephen Farrell
chat.text: I assume there's a mailing list associated with the ITU - that should do for 'em I'd guess

chat.author: Harald Alvestrand
chat.text:
re 2804 reference in previous discussion: note this part from 2804:
"   - On the other hand, the IETF believes that mechanisms designed to
     facilitate or enable wiretapping, or methods of using other
     facilities for such purposes, should be openly described, so as to
     ensure the maximum review of the mechanisms and ensure that they
     adhere as closely as possible to their design constraints. The IETF
     believes that the publication of such mechanisms, and the
     publication of known weaknesses in such mechanisms, is a Good
     Thing.
"

chat.author: Eric Rescorla
chat.text: Like I said, I think the actual description of the various considerations is kind of useful. But if you look at the attempt to apply the taxonomy to an example use case, there's basically a lot of N/A.

chat.author: Erik Nygren
chat.text:
I do think some way of characterizing and factoring the AI drafts would be userful.  It is hard to explain how these various things relate to each other, which makes it hard for us to figure out layering of these things.
I'm not sure that a single-dimensional taxonomy is right either.  For example, this seems multi-dimensional (eg, A2T, A2A, and A2W each have their own needs to address each element in the taxonomy)

Eric Rescorla said:

Martin Thomson said:

Is this really feasible?  FHE is fantastically expensive as a multiplier.  Relative to typical inference workloads which are also fantastically expensive, can this truly be viable?

I understand it's now like 1-2 orders of magnitude, so just slightly infeasible

But it depends on the set of operations that are encoded into the FHE scheme.  I'm no expert on FHE schemes, but I'm not sure how you would encode MCP into an FHE scheme - you have to select your set of operations that are available when you construct this.
